title: Quiz: Microwave Signal Generation
description: Test your understanding of microwave signal generation techniques for quantum control

questions.question: In IQ modulation for qubit control, what do the I and Q components represent?

options.text: The real and imaginary parts of the qubit state
The amplitude and frequency of the pulse
The in-phase and quadrature components that control rotation axis and amplitude
The input and output power levels

questions.explanation: IQ modulation uses the In-phase (I) and Quadrature (Q) components to independently control the rotation axis and amplitude on the Bloch sphere. I controls X rotations while Q controls Y rotations, and their ratio determines the phase of the rotation.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of DRAG (Derivative Removal by Adiabatic Gate) pulses?

options.text: To increase the gate speed
To reduce leakage to higher energy states like |2>
To improve the signal-to-noise ratio
To compensate for mixer imperfections

questions.explanation: DRAG pulses add a derivative correction to the quadrature channel that suppresses transitions to the |2> state. This is critical for transmons where the anharmonicity is only 200-300 MHz, allowing faster gates without significant leakage.

questions.question: For a transmon with -250 MHz anharmonicity, how does the DRAG correction coefficient scale?

options.text: Proportional to the anharmonicity: larger |alpha| means larger correction
Inversely proportional to the anharmonicity: larger |alpha| means smaller correction
Independent of anharmonicity
Proportional to the square of the anharmonicity

questions.explanation: The DRAG coefficient scales as 1/(2*alpha). Larger anharmonicity means better separation between the |1>-|2> transition and the drive frequency, so less correction is needed. Smaller anharmonicity requires larger DRAG corrections.

questions.question: What are the main disadvantages of using IQ mixers compared to direct RF synthesis?

options.text: Higher cost and power consumption
LO leakage, IQ imbalance, and sideband suppression issues requiring calibration
Lower output power
Limited frequency range

questions.explanation: IQ mixers introduce imperfections: LO leakage (carrier feedthrough), IQ imbalance (gain/phase differences between paths), and imperfect sideband cancellation. These require careful calibration and often digital predistortion to correct.

questions.question: Why is phase noise particularly important for qubit control?

options.text: It determines the output power
It causes dephasing of the qubit, directly impacting coherence and gate fidelity
It affects the qubit frequency
It determines the maximum pulse bandwidth

questions.explanation: Phase noise from the local oscillator causes random phase fluctuations in the control signal, which directly translates to dephasing of the qubit. This accumulated phase error reduces gate fidelity, especially for longer gate sequences.

questions.question: In Nyquist zone synthesis, how can you generate a 5 GHz signal using a 2 GSPS DAC?

options.text: It's impossible; the sample rate must exceed 10 GSPS
Generate a 1 GHz IF signal and use the 2nd Nyquist zone image at 3 GHz, then mix up
Generate a 1 GHz IF signal; the image at 2*fs - f_IF = 3 GHz appears in the 2nd Nyquist zone
Use digital interpolation to increase the effective sample rate

questions.explanation: DACs produce images at |n*fs +/- f_IF|. With a 2 GSPS DAC generating 1 GHz IF, the 2nd Nyquist zone image appears at 2*2GHz - 1GHz = 3 GHz. With proper filtering, you could use higher zones to reach 5 GHz, or combine with a mixer stage.

questions.question: For single sideband modulation to generate only the upper sideband at (omega_LO + omega_IF), you set I(t) = cos(omega_IF * t) and Q(t) = sin(omega_IF * t).

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: True! When I(t) = cos(omega_IF*t) and Q(t) = sin(omega_IF*t), the mixer output is I*cos(omega_LO*t) - Q*sin(omega_LO*t) = cos((omega_LO + omega_IF)*t). The lower sideband components cancel out due to the 90-degree phase relationship between I and Q.

questions.question: What phase noise level at 10 kHz offset is typically required for 99.99% gate fidelity?

options.text: Around -60 dBc/Hz
Around -80 dBc/Hz
Around -100 dBc/Hz or better
Around -40 dBc/Hz

questions.explanation: For 99.99% gate fidelity, the integrated phase error must be below ~0.01 radians. This requires phase noise of approximately -100 dBc/Hz at 10 kHz offset or better, depending on the gate duration and integration bandwidth.
